4256.content: This is consistent with the general principle

4267.content: Imagine you're trying to figure out whether a new medicine helps people live lon

4268.content: You have two groups: some people took the medicine and some didn't.

4269.content: The tricky part is that the two groups aren't the same---maybe

4270.content: the medicine group was younger or healthier to begin with.

4271.content: So you can't just compare them directly.

4273.content: One way to fix this is to give each person a ``weight''---a number

4274.content: that makes the two groups look more similar,

4275.content: like putting your thumb on the scale to make a fair comparison.

4276.content: Statisticians have figured out how to do this really well

4277.content: when you only check in on each person once (``did they get better? yes or no'').

4279.content: But in survival analysis, you check in on each person \emph{many} times---every

4280.content: week, or every month---asking ``are they still OK?''

4281.content: That's a lot more data per person, which is great, but it also makes the math

4282.content: harder because now your weights have to work across all those time points,

4286.content: You can pretend you only checked on each person at \emph{one} random time,

4287.content: figure out the weights for that simpler problem,

4288.content: and then use the fact that you actually observed \emph{all} the time points

4290.content: It's like taking a test where you prepared for one random question,

4291.content: but then you get to answer all of them---you'll do at least as well,

4294.content: The math that makes the one-question version work

4295.content: carries over to the all-questions version automatically,

4296.content: which means we get all the same guarantees

4297.content: (the estimates are accurate, the confidence intervals are honest)

4298.content: without having to re-derive everything from scratch.

4300.content: We then use this idea to study counterfactual survival---answering

4301.content: questions like ``how long would these patients have lived

4302.content: if they had taken the medicine?''---and

4303.content: we run simulations to show it works in practice.
